signature_wines.name: Giuseppe Quintarelli Amarone della Valpolicella Classico
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried still red, Amarone della Valpolicella Classico DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Monumental dried cherry, leather and tobacco on the nose; dark plum, espresso, dried orange peel and iron on the palate. Firm, resolved tannins and an exceptionally long finish. The reference-point benchmark Amarone of the Classico zone.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Quintarelli is the undisputed patriarch of Valpolicella. His Amarone, aged seven-plus years in large Slavonian oak and released without filtration, defines what Amarone can be at its most contemplative. No other producer in the appellation matches the combination of breadth, depth and longevity. First bottled in 1958, it has shaped every generation of winemakers in the Classico zone.

signature_wines.name: Dal Forno Romano Amarone della Valpolicella
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried still red, Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Dried black cherry, dark chocolate, espresso and iron minerality on the nose; dried blackberry, bitter chocolate, graphite and tar on the palate. The most extracted, powerful Amarone produced: built for multi-decade cellaring.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Romano Dal Forno transformed Amarone winemaking in the Illasi valley, outside the Classico zone, by applying Burgundy-scale micro-attention to the appassimento method: yields of 500g per vine, four months of temperature-controlled drying, and four years in French barriques. The result attracted near-perfect critical scores and placed the Valpolicella DOC on equal footing with the Classico heartland. His son Marco has continued the approach.

signature_wines.name: Masi Mazzano Amarone della Valpolicella Classico
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried still red, Amarone della Valpolicella Classico DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Black cherry, dried plum, dried rose petal and tobacco on the nose; dark fruit, bitter chocolate, spice and earthy minerality on the palate. Full body with firm, elegant tannins and a long, savoury finish.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Masi's Mazzano is a single-vineyard Amarone from one of the oldest continuously planted plots in the Classico zone. The estate's research arm pioneered the study of Valpolicella's native grape varieties and helped codify the role of Oseleta in the blend. Mazzano represents Masi at its most terroir-focused and is one of the longest-established Amarone crus, first produced in 1964.

signature_wines.name: Bertani Amarone della Valpolicella Classico
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried still red, Amarone della Valpolicella Classico DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Dried cherry, tobacco leaf, leather and dried herbs on the nose; sour plum, cedar, iron, liquorice and a subtle minerality on the palate. Medium-full body with firm, age-integrated tannins. One of the longest-lived Amarones in production.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Bertani has been producing Amarone since the appellation's commercial dawn, their 1958 is the oldest continuously produced Amarone on the market. The house is known for one of the longest ageing regimes: minimum 10 years before release, making each bottle a snapshot of Classico winemaking history. The Corvina-Rondinella simplicity of the blend is itself a deliberate statement of Valpolicella tradition.

signature_wines.name: Allegrini Amarone della Valpolicella Classico Fieramonte
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried still red, Amarone della Valpolicella Classico DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Intense dried cherry, dark rose, tobacco and cedar on the nose; structured dark fruit, bitter chocolate, spice and firm minerality on the palate. Full body, powerful tannins and remarkable ageing potential.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Fieramonte is Allegrini's single-vineyard flagship from the estate's highest Classico plot, first produced in 1983. The Oseleta component, a near-extinct Valpolicella variety that Allegrini helped rescue, gives the wine a colour intensity and tannic grip found in no other Amarone. The estate's research programme into indigenous varieties has had lasting influence on the entire Classico zone.

signature_wines.name: Giuseppe Quintarelli Recioto della Valpolicella Classico
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried sweet red, Recioto della Valpolicella Classico DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Dried cherry, dark chocolate, violets, dried fig and cinnamon on the nose; chocolate-covered cherry, raisin, espresso, liquorice and dried orange peel on the palate. Full body, structured sweetness balanced by firm tannins and vibrant acidity.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Recioto is the ancestor of Amarone, the original prestige wine of Valpolicella before the dry style existed. Quintarelli's Recioto, made only from the prized upper clusters of Corvina and Corvinone, is the appellation's gold standard in its sweet form. Aged six years in large casks, it bridges the zone's ancient sweet-wine tradition with the same patient philosophy that defines his Amarone.

signature_wines.name: Pieropan Soave Classico La Rocca
signature_wines.tasting_notes: White peach, almond blossom, lemon zest and light volcanic mineral on the nose; orchard fruit, saline minerality, almond and a long, clean finish. Single-vineyard purity showing what Garganega reaches on volcanic Classico soils.
signature_wines.why_iconic: La Rocca is the definitive single-vineyard Soave Classico, first produced in 1978 from the steep volcanic terraces of the same name behind Soave town. Leonildo Pieropan was the first producer to vinify Soave as a serious age-worthy wine and to push for geographic single-vineyard recognition within the Classico DOC. La Rocca regularly ranks among Italy's finest whites and is the lens through which Garganega's potential is understood internationally.

signature_wines.name: Pieropan Soave Classico Calvarino
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Ripe white peach, citrus blossom, hay and a hint of fresh almond on the nose; mineral-driven palate with orchard fruit, lemon cream and a precise, clean finish. The Trebbiano di Soave component adds a slight richness and texture.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Calvarino predates La Rocca as Pieropan's first single-vineyard Soave, first produced in 1971, making it one of the longest-documented crus in the appellation. The inclusion of a small percentage of Trebbiano di Soave, the indigenous clone, not common Trebbiano Toscano, gives the wine a character distinct from the pure-Garganega La Rocca. Together the two Pieropan single-vineyard wines set the quality benchmark for all Soave Classico.

signature_wines.name: Inama Bradisismo Soave Superiore DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Concentrated white peach, apricot, fennel flower and volcanic mineral on the nose; rich but precise palate of stone fruit, saline minerality, almond and a long, textured finish. The highest expression of Soave Superiore DOCG terroir.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Bradisismo, named for the slow volcanic ground movement of the Soave hills, is Inama's showcase for what Garganega achieves at the apex of the Soave Superiore DOCG. First made in 2002, it is produced only in exceptional vintages from the estate's oldest volcanic parcels and aged in large oak for texture. It represents the argument that Soave, at its best, is among Italy's most serious white wines.

signature_wines.name: Nino Franco Grave di Stecca Valdobbiadene Superiore di Cartizze
signature_wines.style: Tank-method sparkling white, Valdobbiadene Superiore di Cartizze DOCG
signature_wines.tasting_notes: White peach, pear, acacia blossom and a light creamy note on the nose; delicately sweet, fine persistent bubbles, peach and tropical fruit on the palate with refreshing acidity and a clean, lively finish.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Cartizze is Prosecco's single-vineyard grand cru: 107 hectares of steep amphitheatre slopes above Valdobbiadene that produce the most complex and age-worthy Prosecco. Nino Franco's Grave di Stecca, the estate's oldest Cartizze holding, first produced in 1983, is the benchmark for what Cartizze should taste like. Nino Franco was instrumental in establishing Valdobbiadene's international reputation and pushing for the DOCG classification.

signature_wines.name: Adami Vigneto Giardino Rive di Colbertaldo Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore Brut
signature_wines.style: Tank-method sparkling white, Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG Rive di Colbertaldo
signature_wines.vintage_range: 2009-present (Rive designation introduced with the 2010 DOCGregulations, released from specific vintages)
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Fresh white peach, pear, acacia and a light floral note on the nose; clean, lively mousse, crisp orchard fruit, citrus and mineral on the palate. Brut dosage accentuates the wine's precision over sweetness.
signature_wines.why_iconic: The Rive designation, introduced in 2010 to identify single-village or single-vineyard Prosecco Superiore crus, is Valdobbiadene's quality signpost for the future. Adami's Vigneto Giardino from the Rive di Colbertaldo is one of the most critically recognised and consumer-known Rive bottlings, demonstrating that Prosecco from a named hillside plot has terroir character distinct from generic DOC production. It anchors the breadth of Veneto's sparkling wine tradition beyond Cartizze.

signature_wines.name: Maculan Torcolato Breganze
signature_wines.style: Appassimento-dried sweet white, Breganze Torcolato DOC
signature_wines.tasting_notes: Apricot jam, dried peach, honey, orange blossom and light saffron on the nose; luscious sweet palate of preserved citrus, almond, dried apricot and warm spice, balanced by the Vespaiola grape's natural acidity. A rich but never heavy dessert white.
signature_wines.why_iconic: Torcolato is Veneto's only world-class sweet white made from the rare Vespaiola grape, produced in tiny quantities in the Breganze DOC north of Vicenza. Fausto Maculan resurrected the nearly-extinct style in 1974, the traditional name comes from the roped (torcolato) way grapes were hung to dry, and turned it into an internationally recognised dessert wine. It is one of Italy's most original and territorially specific sweet wines outside of Passito di Pantelleria or Vin Santo.
